Move your tools out of your application, keep your permission model intact, and let your AI discover capabilities dynamically.
As AI applications become more sophisticated, one problem appears repeatedly:
How do we keep adding tools without turning our AI harness into a giant collection of hardcoded functions?
The answer many developers are exploring is Model Context Protocol (MCP).
Despite the hype surrounding MCP, its core idea is surprisingly simple. Underneath the branding, it is essentially a lightweight protocol that allows an AI host application to discover and execute tools from external servers.
In this article, we’ll build the mental model behind an MCP-powered AI harness, understand what actually changes in your codebase, implement a practical TypeScript example, and discuss why permission management should never belong to the MCP server.
The Problem with Hardcoded AI Tools
A typical AI application often starts like this:
AI Harness
│
├── searchFiles()
├── calculateInvoice()
├── sendEmail()
├── createTicket()
├── summarizeDocument()
└── ...
